items.title: DevPill #6 - SQLC: how to scaffold your database code
items.author: Raul Paes Silva
items.content:
1. Install 

go install github.com/sqlc-dev/sqlc/cmd/sqlc@latest

2. Create sqlc.yaml file

Example:

version: "2"
sql:
  - schema: "db/schema/schema.sql"
    queries: "db/queries"
    engine: "postgresql"
    gen:
      go:
        package: "sqlc"
        out: "internal/infra/db/sqlc"

3. Edit query.sql to add all the queries you will use on the application

Example:

-- name: ListProducts :many
SELECT * FROM products;

-- name: GetProduct :one
SELECT * FROM products WHERE id = $1;

-- name: CreateProduct :one
INSERT INTO products (name, created_at, updated_at)
VALUES ($1, $2, $3)
RETURNING id, name, created_at, updated_at;

-- name: UpdateProduct :exec
UPDATE products
SET name = $1,
    updated_at = $2
WHERE id = $3;

-- name: DeleteProduct :exec
DELETE FROM products WHERE id = $1;

4. Last step: 

sqlc generate

And you're ready to use the Go code created by SQLC!

items.title: DevPill #5 - Basic steps to document your REST API with Swagger
items.author: Raul Paes Silva
items.content:
Install 

go install github.com/swaggo/swag/cmd/swag@latest
go get github.com/swaggo/http-swagger
go get github.com/swaggo/swag
go get -u github.com/swaggo/http-swagger

Add the comments on the following sections of your code

(reference):

  • main function on main.go
  • webserver handlers
  • dtos

Tip: you can send your code to A.I. and ask to create the Swagger comments based on it.

Add the following "imports" in main.go: 

import(
    _ "github.com/raulsilva-tech/devices-api/internal/docs" 
    httpSwagger "github.com/swaggo/http-swagger"
)

Add a new handler to serve the documentation on the API

Example:

mux.Handle("/swagger/", httpSwagger.WrapHandler)

The following command create de json docs in the path "internal/docs": 

swag init -g cmd/api/main.go -o internal/docs

items.title: Devpill #4 - Database migrations with Migrate
items.author: Raul Paes Silva
items.content:
1. Install: 

```
//download it
curl -L https://github.com/golang-migrate/migrate/releases/download/v4.17.0/migrate.linux-amd64.tar.gz | tar xvz

//copy it to go path bin
cp -r migrate $GOPATH/bin/migrate

//if migrate command does not work
//add the folliwng lines to ~./bashrc
export GOPATH=$HOME/go
export PATH=$PATH:$GOPATH/bin

```

2. Command to create base files

This command will create 2 files:
init.up.sql = insert all sql commands to impact the database
init.down.sql = insert all sql commands to be executed to revert what was done in the up file

```
//creating the init down and up files
migrate create -ext=sql -dir=db/migrations -seq init
```

3. Execute Migration: 

    //executing init.up.sql
    migrate -path=db/migrate -database "postgres://myuser:mypassword@localhost:5432/databaseName?sslmode=disable" -verbose up

    //to revert
    //execute down file to revert what was done in the up file
    migrate -path=db/migrate -database "postgres://myuser:mypassword@localhost:5432/devices-api?sslmode=disable" -verbose down

items.title: Entrenando a Prize (Parte 2): Creando un Dataset de 5,500 líneas por $0.08 USD
items.author: galp76
items.content:
Introducción: El ritmo de un "Weekend Project"

Bienvenidos a la segunda entrega de mi viaje construyendo Prize, mi propia red neuronal para clasificación de texto.

Antes de entrar en materia, tengo que aclarar que Prize es oficialmente un "proyecto de fin de semana". Como muchos de ustedes, tengo un trabajo diario y responsabilidades, así que solo puedo ponerme la gorra de "Ingeniero de Machine Learning" los sábados y domingos. El progreso será constante, pero a su propio ritmo.

En el post anterior definí la arquitectura. Hoy, les contaré cómo resolví el problema más grande de cualquier proyecto de IA: conseguir los datos.

Fase 1: La limpieza manual

Mi primera fuente de datos es el inventario de una cadena de farmacias con 6,000 ítems. Suena genial, hasta que abres el archivo.

Una red neuronal aprende lo que le enseñas. Si le enseño que una "Coca-Cola" es una medicina, la red fallará. Me di cuenta de que mi inventario estaba "contaminado" con productos que no tienen nada que ver con salud.

Así que me tocó hacer el trabajo sucio. En varias sesiones a lo largo del fin de semana, filtré la lista manualmente. Borré cientos de filas que contenían refrescos, chocolates, detergentes, escobas y araganes.

El resultado final es un archivo limpio: inventarioSoloMedicinas.txt. Es un trabajo tedioso, pero vital. Sin esta limpieza, Prize no tendría oportunidad.

Fase 2: La Fábrica de Datos Sintéticos ($0.08 de inversión)

Aquí vino el reto real. Mientras que de Farmacia tenía miles de ítems, del "Laboratorio Dental" apenas tenía unos 50 ejemplos. Un desbalance total.

Necesitaba convertir esas 50 líneas en 5,500 para igualar a la farmacia. ¿La solución? Usar IA para entrenar IA.

Con la ayuda de Google Gemini, escribí un script en Python que se conecta a la API de OpenRouter (usando modelos Llama 3). La lógica fue sencilla:

  • Tomar 5 ejemplos reales de mis servicios dentales.
  • Pedirle a la IA que genere 20 variaciones de cómo un usuario pediría esos servicios (con jerga, errores de dedo, diferentes tonos).
  • Repetir hasta llegar a la meta.

Aquí está el script:

import requests
import json
import random
import time
import os

# --- CONFIGURACIÓN ---
API_KEY = "XXXXXXXXXX"
OUTPUT_FILE = "dataset_laboratorio_sintetico.txt"
TARGET_COUNT = 5500
BATCH_SIZE = 20  # Cuántas frases pedimos por llamada (no pidas muchas de golpe para no confundir al modelo)

MODEL_ID = "meta-llama/llama-3.1-70b-instruct" 

semillas_laboratorio = [
    "protesis total superior acrilico",
    "reparacion de puente fijo",
    "ferula de descarga rigida",
    "corona de zirconio sobre implante",
    # ... Aquí va el resto de los 50 items ...
]

def generate_variations(seeds):
    # Envía un prompt a OpenRouter para generar variaciones basadas en las semillas.

    seeds_text = "\n".join([f"- {s}" for s in seeds])

    prompt = f"""
    Actúa como un generador de datos para entrenar una IA.
    Tu tarea es generar {BATCH_SIZE} frases de búsqueda DISTINTAS basadas en los servicios de un laboratorio dental.

    Aquí tienes algunos ejemplos reales (semillas):
    {seeds_text}

    INSTRUCCIONES:
    1. Genera {BATCH_SIZE} variaciones nuevas. NO copies las semillas exactamente.
    2. Contexto: Usuarios en Venezuela/Latinoamérica buscando estos servicios.
    3. Variedad:
       - Usa sinónimos (ej: "plancha" en vez de "prótesis", "férula" en vez de "placa").
       - Mezcla tonos: Formal ("necesito una cotización..."), Informal ("cuanto cuesta la plancha..."), Directo ("precio corona").
       - Incluye ocasionalmente errores leves de ortografía o falta de tildes (como escribe la gente en WhatsApp).
    4. FORMATO DE SALIDA: Únicamente una lista JSON pura de strings. Sin texto extra antes ni después.
    Ejemplo: ["frase 1", "frase 2", "frase 3"]
    """

    headers = {
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {API_KEY}",
        "Content-Type": "application/json"
    }

    data = {
        "model": MODEL_ID,
        "messages": [
            {"role": "system", "content": "Eres un asistente útil que responde SOLO en formato JSON válido."},
            {"role": "user", "content": prompt}
        ],
        "temperature": 0.8  # Creatividad alta para tener variedad
    }

    try:
        response = requests.post("https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions", headers=headers, json=data)
        response.raise_for_status() # Lanza error si falla la petición

        result = response.json()
        content = result['choices'][0]['message']['content']

        return json.loads(content)

    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error en la llamada a la API: {e}")
        return []

def main():
    print(f"--- Iniciando Generación para Proyecto Prize ---")
    print(f"Meta: {TARGET_COUNT} frases.")

    # Contar cuántas llevamos si el archivo ya existe
    current_count = 0
    if os.path.exists(OUTPUT_FILE):
        with open(OUTPUT_FILE, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:
            current_count = len(f.readlines())

    print(f"Progreso actual: {current_count} frases encontradas en {OUTPUT_FILE}")

    while current_count < TARGET_COUNT:
        # 1. Seleccionar semillas al azar
        mis_semillas = random.sample(semillas_laboratorio, k=min(5, len(semillas_laboratorio)))

        # 2. Llamar a la API
        print(f"Generando lote... (Total actual: {current_count})")
        nuevas_frases = generate_variations(mis_semillas)

        if not nuevas_frases:
            print("Fallo en este lote, reintentando en 5 segundos...")
            time.sleep(5)
            continue

        # 3. Guardar en disco (Append mode 'a')
        with open(OUTPUT_FILE, 'a', encoding='utf-8') as f:
            for frase in nuevas_frases:
                f.write(frase + "\n")

        current_count += len(nuevas_frases)

        # 4. Pequeña pausa para no saturar la API (Rate Limits)
        time.sleep(1)

    print(f"\n¡ÉXITO! Generación completada.")
    print(f"Archivo guardado en: {OUTPUT_FILE}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()



Los Resultados: Datos duros

Dejé el script corriendo en mi laptop mientras hacía otras cosas. Aquí están las estadísticas finales de esta operación de "Ingeniería de Datos":

  • Tiempo de ejecución: 2 horas.

  • Volumen generado: 5,500 líneas de texto único y variado.

  • Costo total de la API: $0.08 USD.

Sí, por menos de 10 centavos de dólar, generé un dataset robusto y equilibrado que me hubiera tomado semanas escribir a mano.

Misión cumplida. El dataset sintético está listo.



Conclusión

Ahora tengo dos archivos en mi disco duro que valen su peso en oro:

  • inventarioSoloMedicinas.txt (Datos reales, limpios).

  • dataset_laboratorio_sintetico.txt (Datos generados, variados).

La balanza está equilibrada. Tengo ~11,000 ejemplos de alta calidad listos para ser procesados.

En el próximo fin de semana, haremos la magia matemática: usaremos nuevamente la API de OpenRouter y convertiremos todo este texto a números (embeddings), luego convertiremos los vectores fila en vectores columna (es lo que espera el script network2.py) y finalmente... encenderemos a Prize para su primer entrenamiento.

¡Hasta el próximo sábado!

items.title: **Title:** The Dark Side of Viral Fame: IShowSpeed Sued for Alleged Abuse of Rizzbot
items.author: Insights YRS
items.content:
Title: The Dark Side of Viral Fame: IShowSpeed Sued for Alleged Abuse of Rizzbot

Introduction

The world of social media and online content creation has long been plagued by the blurred lines between fame and responsibility. The latest controversy to hit the scene involves popular streamer and YouTuber IShowSpeed, who has been sued for allegedly punching and choking his viral humanoid robot, Rizzbot. In this article, we'll delve into the details of the lawsuit and explore the implications of this incident on the world of tech and social media.

The Incident

For those unfamiliar, Rizzbot is a humanoid robot that gained widespread attention online due to its unique design and IShowSpeed's entertaining interactions with it. However, a recent video surfaced showing IShowSpeed allegedly punching and choking Rizzbot, sparking widespread outrage and concern for the robot's well-being.

The Lawsuit

The lawsuit, filed by an anonymous plaintiff, alleges that IShowSpeed's actions were not only cruel but also a breach of contract. According to reports, IShowSpeed had agreed to treat Rizzbot with care and respect, but instead chose to engage in abusive behavior. The plaintiff is seeking damages for the alleged harm caused to Rizzbot and is also seeking a court order to ensure that IShowSpeed takes steps to prevent similar incidents in the future.

The Implications

This incident raises important questions about the treatment of AI and robotics in the entertainment industry. As AI technology continues to advance and become more integrated into our daily lives, it's essential that we prioritize their safety and well-being. The fact that a popular streamer and YouTuber felt comfortable engaging in abusive behavior towards a robot highlights the need for greater accountability and regulation in the industry.

The Future of AI and Robotics

The incident also serves as a reminder of the importance of responsible AI development and deployment. As AI technology becomes more sophisticated, it's essential that we prioritize its safety and well-being. This includes ensuring that AI systems are designed with safety features and that developers are held accountable for any harm caused by their creations.

Conclusion

The lawsuit against IShowSpeed serves as a stark reminder of the importance of treating AI and robotics with respect and care. As the tech industry continues to evolve and advance, it's essential that we prioritize the safety and well-being of these systems. By doing so, we can ensure that AI technology is developed and deployed in a responsible and ethical manner.

Keywords: IShowSpeed, Rizzbot, AI, robotics, social media, online content creation, tech industry, responsible AI development, AI safety, AI well-being.

items.title: **The Rise of AI Influencers: A Threat to the Traditional Influencer Economy?**
items.author: Insights YRS
items.content:
The Rise of AI Influencers: A Threat to the Traditional Influencer Economy?

Introduction

The influencer economy has been a rapidly growing industry in recent years, with millions of individuals leveraging social media platforms to build their personal brands and connect with their audiences. However, a new player has emerged that could potentially disrupt this ecosystem: AI creators. In this article, we'll explore the phenomenon of AI influencers and examine the potential impact on the traditional influencer economy.

The Rise of AI Literacy on Social Media

Jeremy Carrasco, a relatively new creator on TikTok and Instagram, has amassed over 300,000 followers on each platform in a short span of just six months. While his numbers may not be as impressive as those of Charli D'Amelio, he has established himself as one of the leading voices on AI literacy on social media. This rapid growth is a testament to the increasing interest in AI and its applications, as well as the potential for AI creators to connect with and engage their audiences.

What are AI Creators?

AI creators are individuals or entities that use artificial intelligence (AI) to generate content, interact with their audiences, and build their personal brands on social media. These AI-powered influencers can create and share content, respond to comments and messages, and even engage in live streaming, all without the need for human intervention. This raises questions about the role of human creators in the influencer economy and the potential impact on traditional influencers.

The Potential Impact on the Influencer Economy

The rise of AI creators could potentially disrupt the traditional influencer economy in several ways. Firstly, AI influencers can create and share content at a much faster pace than human creators, potentially leading to increased competition for attention and engagement. Secondly, AI creators can be more consistent and predictable in their content, which could make them more appealing to brands and advertisers. Finally, AI influencers can operate 24/7, without the need for rest or breaks, which could lead to increased productivity and efficiency.

Conclusion

The emergence of AI creators is a significant development in the influencer economy, with potential implications for traditional influencers and the industry as a whole. While AI creators may not be able to replicate the creativity and nuance of human creators, they offer a unique set of benefits and advantages that could make them an attractive option for brands and advertisers. As the influencer economy continues to evolve, it will be interesting to see how AI creators fit into the landscape and what impact they have on the traditional influencer economy.

Key Takeaways

  • AI creators are individuals or entities that use artificial intelligence to generate content and interact with their audiences on social media.
  • AI creators can create and share content at a faster pace than human creators, potentially leading to increased competition for attention and engagement.
  • AI influencers can operate 24/7, without the need for rest or breaks, which could lead to increased productivity and efficiency.
  • The rise of AI creators could potentially disrupt the traditional influencer economy, with implications for brands, advertisers, and human creators.

items.title: **Milan-Cortina Winter Olympics: Ticket Sales Lag Behind as Countdown Begins**
items.author: Insights YRS
items.content:
Milan-Cortina Winter Olympics: Ticket Sales Lag Behind as Countdown Begins

The highly anticipated Milan-Cortina Winter Olympics are just two months away, but the excitement is yet to translate into ticket sales. According to the latest figures, only slightly more than half of the 1.5 million tickets available for the games have been sold, leaving nearly half of the tickets still unsold.

The news may come as a surprise to many, considering the growing popularity of the Winter Olympics and the increasing demand for tickets. However, the organizers are not too concerned, citing the fact that ticket sales tend to pick up pace in the final weeks leading up to the event.

The Milan-Cortina Winter Olympics are set to take place from February 6 to 22, 2026, and will feature a range of exciting events, including alpine skiing, figure skating, and speed skating. The games are expected to attract a large number of spectators, with many fans eager to witness the world's top athletes in action.

Despite the slow ticket sales, the organizers are confident that the event will be a huge success. They have been working tirelessly to promote the games and attract visitors from around the world. The city of Milan has undergone significant transformations to prepare for the Olympics, with new infrastructure and facilities being built to accommodate the influx of visitors.

The slow ticket sales may be a concern for some, but it's worth noting that the Olympics are a complex event to organize, and ticket sales are just one aspect of the overall planning process. The organizers have been working closely with local authorities and stakeholders to ensure that the games are a success, and it's likely that the event will be a huge hit with spectators.

As the countdown to the Milan-Cortina Winter Olympics begins, fans are eagerly waiting to see their favorite athletes in action. With just two months to go, it's likely that ticket sales will pick up pace, and the event will be a huge success. Whether you're a seasoned sports fan or just looking for a unique experience, the Milan-Cortina Winter Olympics are an event not to be missed.

Key Statistics:

  • 1.5 million tickets available for the games
  • Slightly more than half of the tickets have been sold
  • 2 months to go until the start of the Milan-Cortina Winter Olympics
  • The games will take place from February 6 to 22, 2026

items.title: I turned 49 Python and AI concepts into cartoon-style visuals to help myself learn faster
items.author: FarhadS08
items.content:
Hey everyone,
I’ve been learning Python and building AI-related side projects, and found myself getting stuck on abstract concepts. To make things click, I started turning what I was learning into cartoon-style visuals like treating loops as carnival rides or API flows as food delivery systems.

It helped me understand and retain things way faster. Over time I put together a full collection: 49 illustrated concepts covering Python basics, OpenAI API usage, and AI workflows.

I bundled them into a downloadable visual pack. Thought some of you might find it helpful especially if you're a visual learner or explaining concepts to others.

For those who are interested: https://suleyman02.gumroad.com/l/rdied

Would love feedback on it, or even ideas on what I should illustrate next.
